meta.title: Level 2: The Briefing
meta.description: Learn to talk to Claude Code like you are briefing a capable but context-free colleague.

blocks.content:
## Welcome to Level 2

In Level 1 you learned that Claude Code is an autonomous agent running a gather-act-verify loop. Now comes the question that determines whether that loop produces great work or mediocre work: **what did you say to start it?**

Most people dramatically underinvest in their initial prompt. They type something vague, get a vague result, then spend five follow-up messages trying to steer Claude toward what they actually wanted. This is backwards. A two-minute investment in a clear briefing saves twenty minutes of correction.

The mental model for Level 2 is the **briefing**. Imagine you are handing a task to a smart colleague who just joined your team. They are capable and motivated, but they know nothing about your project, your preferences, or your definition of "done." What would you write in a briefing document to set them up for success?

That is exactly what your prompt to Claude Code should be.

### What you will learn

| Module | Topic | Core Idea |
| :-- | :-- | :-- |
| 2.1 | The Briefing Mindset | Define what done looks like, not how to get there |
| 2.2 | Specificity Is Kindness | Precise constraints beat verbose explanations |
| 2.3 | Give Something to Verify | The single highest-leverage habit |
| 2.4 | The Interview Pattern | Let Claude ask you the clarifying questions |
| 2.5 | Mentions and Piping | Getting the right briefing materials to Claude |
| 2.6 | Knowing When to Reset | Correction spirals vs. starting fresh |
| 2.7 | Checkpoint | Rewrite bad prompts into effective briefings |

The quality of Claude's output is directly proportional to the quality of your briefing. Think of your prompt as a document you are handing to a capable but context-free colleague — define what done looks like, provide the right materials, and set clear success criteria.

blocks.prompt: Before you start Level 2, think about the last time you gave someone instructions and the result was not what you expected. Was the problem their competence, or was it your briefing? Keep that example in mind as you work through these modules.
